identity.title: Chocolate and Confectionery Manufacturing from Cacao Beans

lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers that shell, roast, or grind cacao beans and make chocolate, cacao products, or chocolate confectionery for repeat wholesale, private-label, foodservice, or retail-channel customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Purchased-chocolate confectioners, nonchocolate candy makers, immediate-consumption shops, pure retailers, captive internal plants, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat product sales under wholesale, distributor, private-label, foodservice, and retailer relationships; revenue is primarily per unit or shipment rather than a cost-plus service fee.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader food-manufacturing occupation mix, where production and nonsupervisory workers are about four-fifths of employment and common roles include batchmakers and packaging-machine operators. Census measures upper-bound exposure to all advanced automation in manufacturing, not remaining AI task exposure in cacao-based confectionery. I treat cognitive planning, visual inspection, documentation, scheduling, and administrative portions as AI-exposed while discounting physical line work; the broad automation measure likely overstates AI-only exposure.
a.rationale: The industry's core transformation of beans into food is equipment- and process-heavy. AI can reduce labor in inspection triage, demand and production planning, traceability documentation, customer service, and back office, but cannot directly replace most sanitation, maintenance, material movement, or accountable line operation within five years.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit staffing matrix with wages by occupation, paired with time-and-motion interviews at independent bean-to-bar and industrial chocolate plants and task-level AI deployment data, would materially change the range.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for NAICS 311, not 311351.
Advanced-technology exposure includes robotics and dedicated equipment, not just AI, and is explicitly an upper bound.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures AI use across all nonfarm employer businesses and reports higher use at larger firms, while ABS evidence covers technology adoption across businesses and manufacturing rather than this six-digit industry. I translate adoption and the need for workflow changes into a five-year implementation share, reducing it for food-safety validation, integration with legacy plant controls, and small-firm capital and data constraints; broad business adoption likely overstates deployability in small food plants.
rho.rationale: Repeatable office and planning workflows should be implementable, but plant-floor changes require validated controls, reliable sensor data, human quality accountability, and integration with existing equipment. Implementation therefore captures only a minority of the exposed opportunity in the base case.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level case studies reporting before-and-after labor hours from AI vision, production planning, predictive maintenance, and quality-documentation deployments at small confectionery manufacturers would materially move this estimate.
caveats: Survey definitions of AI changed over time and include any business function.
Large-company deployment experience may not transfer to LMM plants.

e.rationale: Most operating manufacturers in the code sell repeat physical output to external customers and fit the operating-business lens. Eligibility is reduced for captive facilities, firms dominated by direct immediate-consumption retail, owner-dependent microbusinesses, and operations without transferable customer relationships or food-safety systems.
e.change_evidence: A review of the SUSB-derived LMM candidate list using establishment websites, customer concentration, ownership, channel mix, EBITDA normalization, and captive-status checks would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: The frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the EBITDA band, so this eligibility share has no effect unless that estimate is revised.
Census reports establishments, not eligible control-transfer firms.

s5.bridge: Gallup surveyed US business owners across industries about plans for the next five years and found 17% of owners age 55 or older planned a sale or other ownership transfer, versus 10% of younger owners. Project Equity separately reports that 60% of small and midsize manufacturers have owners at or near retirement age. I blend these cross-industry intentions, allow for manufacturing's older ownership, and discount intentions that become gifts, internal reorganizations, closures, or failed sales; the manufacturer age proxy could overstate completed qualifying transfers.
s5.rationale: An aging manufacturing-owner base creates transfer pressure, but planned exits do not all become completed, externally transferable control transactions. Specialized facilities, commodity risk, and buyer financing further constrain completion.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data from Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, or IBBA filtered to confectionery/food manufacturers and $1-10M EBITDA, matched to the eligible-firm denominator over five years, would materially improve this probability.
caveats: Neither source isolates NAICS 311351 or the stated EBITDA band.
Intentions are not completed transactions, and gifts may not qualify as an external control transfer.

q.bridge: Hershey's 2025 filing and results measure pricing and volume for a large branded public confectioner, not retention of AI benefits at LMM cacao processors. Favorable price realization alongside elasticity-driven volume decline demonstrates meaningful pricing power but also customer response. I infer moderate retention for fixed unit-price sales, with branded or differentiated firms above private-label and commodity processors; the large-brand proxy likely overstates LMM retention.
q.rationale: Benefits are not mechanically passed through because contracts and shelf prices are generally unit-based, so an efficient operator can initially retain savings. Retailer bargaining, renewals, private-label bids, competitor matching, and consumer price elasticity should share a meaningful portion over five years.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, bid histories, gross-margin bridges, price realization, and renewal data from independent chocolate manufacturers segmented by branded, private-label, and ingredient channels would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Hershey has brand strength and scale unlike most LMM firms.
Observed pricing responses reflect cocoa inflation and broader productivity, not AI savings alone.

d5.bridge: NCA measures the total US confectionery category, not constant-price demand for cacao-bean manufacturers, and forecasts nominal retail sales from $55 billion in 2025 to $62.2 billion in 2030. It also reports 99.8% household penetration but acknowledges inflation boosted dollars while unit and volume sales struggled. BLS projects food-processing-equipment-worker employment up 5% from 2024 to 2034. I strip out nominal price growth and halve the ten-year broad-food labor signal to anchor roughly flat five-year real quantity; the nominal category forecast would overstate constant-price demand.
d5.rationale: Chocolate is a deeply penetrated, repeat-purchase category with strong seasonal demand, but mature consumption, price elasticity, cocoa-driven price increases, and substitution toward nonchocolate candy limit real volume growth. A near-flat base is more defensible than applying nominal sales growth.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Circana or NIQ unit-volume data and Euromonitor constant-price forecasts split between chocolate made from cacao beans, purchased-chocolate confectionery, and nonchocolate candy would materially change this ratio.
caveats: Category evidence mixes manufacturing codes and channels.
The NCA forecast is nominal and partly reflects inflation; conversion to constant-price quantity is judgmental.

o.rationale: The service basket is physical food production: beans must still be procured, transformed, tested, packaged, traced, and delivered by an accountable regulated operator. Software may consolidate planning and intermediation, but it cannot eliminate the manufacturer; only limited self-manufacture, channel bypass, or substitution to purchased chocolate removes this operator role.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy surveys, private-label insourcing announcements, plant closure and vertical-integration data, and channel-specific procurement interviews would test how much volume could bypass independent cacao processors.
caveats: The estimate does not separately model consolidation into large manufacturers.
A shift from bean processing to purchased chocolate would move activity to NAICS 311352 even if confectionery demand persists.

narrative.executive_view: Cacao-bean chocolate manufacturing has durable repeat demand and real pricing discretion, but it is fundamentally a physical, regulated production business. The largest AI opportunities sit around the line rather than replacing it, and the frozen firm-count estimate identifies no firms in the target EBITDA band.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve visual-inspection triage, production scheduling, demand forecasting, maintenance prioritization, traceability records, customer service, and administrative work. Sanitation, material handling, process control, maintenance, and final food-quality accountability remain human-and-equipment intensive, so exposed labor and five-year implementation are bounded.
narrative.value_capture: Unit-based product pricing lets operators retain some productivity benefit, especially where products or customer service are differentiated. Retailer leverage, private-label rebids, competitive pricing, and volume elasticity gradually share the gains; large branded-company pricing power is an optimistic proxy for independent firms.
narrative.firm_availability: Manufacturing owners skew older and cross-industry survey evidence shows meaningful five-year transfer intent. Yet intent does not equal a completed qualifying control sale, and specialized plants can be hard to finance or transfer. More importantly, the injected size-band estimate is zero, so candidate existence is the immediate diligence question.
narrative.demand_durability: Confectionery reaches nearly every household and seasonal occasions support recurring consumption. Reported dollar growth is heavily price-driven while volume has struggled, so constant-price five-year demand is best treated as roughly flat rather than extrapolating nominal forecasts.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal evidence gaps are six-digit occupation data, LMM AI deployment outcomes, private-company pricing and renewal behavior, and completed transfer rates. Cocoa volatility, retailer concentration, food-safety failures, customer concentration, branded versus private-label mix, and migration to purchased chocolate can dominate small productivity gains.
narrative.principal_driver: Persistent repeat consumer demand for physical chocolate products keeps the accountable manufacturing operator necessary.
narrative.principal_weakness: The frozen LMM count is zero and the labor base is dominated by physical plant work with limited AI-only substitution.

sources.title: 311351: Chocolate and Confectionery Manufacturing from Cacao Beans - Census Bureau Profile
sources.supports: Industry definition, cross-references, and 280 employer establishments in 2023.

sources.title: Food Manufacturing: NAICS 311
sources.supports: Broader food-manufacturing employment and occupation mix, including production/nonsupervisory employment and common batchmaking and packaging roles.

sources.title: Food Processing Equipment Workers
sources.supports: Physical duties, 78% employment in food manufacturing, automatic weighing and mixing, and 5% projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034.

sources.title: Three Results From Recent Research on Advanced Technology Use and Automation
sources.supports: Manufacturing worker exposure to advanced automating technologies was 52% versus 28% outside manufacturing, explicitly described as an upper bound.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: BTOS AI usage of 17-20% overall in late 2025 through May 2026, expected use of 20-23%, and higher use at larger firms.

sources.title: Census Bureau's 2023 Annual Business Survey Provides Insight into Technology Adoption by Businesses
sources.supports: Most adopting businesses reported no overall worker-number change; AI and other technologies often required workflow and skill changes.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 survey of five-year owner plans: 17% of owners age 55 or older versus 10% of younger owners planned sale or other ownership transfer.

sources.title: Employee Ownership for Manufacturers
sources.supports: Reported that 60% of small and midsize manufacturers have owners at or near retirement age.

sources.title: The Hershey Company 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Large branded confectioner evidence on price realization, volume elasticity, raw-material pricing, and lag from list-price increases to net sales.

sources.title: Confectionery Sales Climb to $55 Billion in 2025
sources.supports: 2025 US confectionery retail sales, 99.8% household purchase penetration, chocolate share, seasonal concentration, and nominal forecast to 2030.
